title: What Business Ethics Is
simple_html:
Business ethics is the study of what is right and wrong in the world of commerce. It asks how companies and the people inside them should behave — not just what the law requires, but what is morally good and fair.

It matters because business decisions affect real people: employees, customers, suppliers, communities, and investors. A choice that is legal can still be unethical (for example, exploiting a loophole), and ethics is the tool we use to judge those gray areas.

Two key distinctions

Values vs. ethics: Values are the things you care about (honesty, loyalty, profit). Ethics are the standards and reasoning you use to decide which actions are acceptable. Values motivate; ethics evaluate.

Descriptive vs. normative: Descriptive ethics simply describes what people actually believe and do. Normative ethics argues what people ought to do. Business ethics is mostly normative — it prescribes.

Key takeaways

  • Ethics goes beyond the law — legal does not always mean right.
  • Values are what we care about; ethics is how we reason about right action.
  • The field is mainly normative (what should be) rather than just descriptive (what is).
  • Ethical questions appear at three levels: the individual, the organization, and the system (markets, society).
  • Good ethics is also good for business long-term: trust, reputation, and reduced risk.

In short, business ethics gives managers a structured way to think through hard choices where money, people, and principle collide.
intermediate_html:
Business ethics applies moral reasoning to the conduct of organizations and the individuals within them. It is the discipline that helps managers move from "What can we do?" to "What should we do?"

Defining the field

Business ethics examines moral standards as they apply to business policies, institutions, and behavior. It operates at three levels: the individual (a manager padding an expense report), the organizational (a firm’s pollution policy), and the systemic (whether capitalism distributes wealth fairly). Most workplace dilemmas blend all three.

Values vs. ethics

Values are enduring beliefs about what is desirable — honesty, fairness, achievement, security. They are personal and cultural. Ethics is the systematic reasoning we use to determine which actions are right or wrong. A person can value loyalty (a value) yet face an ethical question about whether loyalty to a boss justifies covering up fraud. Values supply the raw material; ethics supplies the judgment.

Descriptive vs. normative ethics

This distinction is exam-critical. Descriptive ethics is empirical: it reports what people and cultures actually believe and do (a survey of how often employees lie). Normative ethics is prescriptive: it argues what people ought to do and why, using frameworks like utilitarianism or Kantian duty. A third branch, metaethics, asks what moral terms even mean (is "good" objective or just opinion?). Business ethics draws on all three but is dominated by normative analysis.

Ethics vs. the law

Law sets a floor, not a ceiling. The relationship has four cells: actions that are legal and ethical (most business), legal but unethical (price-gouging during a crisis), illegal but arguably ethical (civil disobedience, some whistleblowing), and illegal and unethical (fraud). Managers get into trouble when they treat "it’s legal" as the end of the conversation.

Worked mini-case

A pharmaceutical firm prices a life-saving drug at $1,000 per dose because the market will bear it and patents make it legal. Descriptively, competitors do the same. Normatively, we ask whether the harm to patients who cannot pay is justified by the value of innovation incentives. The firm’s stated value ("patients first") clashes with its pricing. Business ethics forces the firm to confront that gap rather than hide behind legality.

How to apply it

  1. Identify the level — individual, organizational, or systemic? This tells you who can act.
  2. Separate values from ethics — name the values in tension, then reason about the right action.
  3. Check law as a floor, not a ceiling — ask the "legal but unethical" question explicitly.
  4. Stay normative — don’t excuse a choice just because "everyone does it" (the descriptive trap, or appeal to common practice).

Mastering this lesson sets up the normative frameworks that follow — consequentialism, deontology, virtue ethics, and justice — each a different lens for answering "what should we do?"
full_html:
Business ethics is the branch of applied ethics that studies the moral dimensions of commercial activity — the principles, standards, and reasoning that determine right and wrong conduct in organizations. It is both an academic discipline and a practical managerial competence. This lesson establishes the vocabulary and distinctions on which the rest of the course depends.

Scope and levels of analysis

Ethical issues in business arise at three nested levels (Crane & Matten, 2016). At the individual level, employees face dilemmas of honesty, loyalty, and conflict of interest. At the organizational level, firms set policies on safety, marketing, environmental impact, and labor. At the systemic level, we evaluate the morality of economic institutions themselves — markets, property rights, and the distribution of wealth. A single event, such as a factory closure, can be analyzed at all three: the manager who decides, the firm that profits, and the system that permits offshoring. Velasquez (2012) emphasizes that confusing these levels leads to misplaced blame.

Values versus ethics

Values are stable, internalized beliefs about what is desirable or worth pursuing — Rokeach (1973) catalogued these as terminal values (end-states like freedom or prosperity) and instrumental values (modes of conduct like honesty or ambition). Ethics, by contrast, is the systematic, defensible reasoning about which actions are morally permissible, obligatory, or forbidden. The relationship is generative but not identical: values motivate behavior, while ethics evaluates it. Two people may share the value of "loyalty" yet reach opposite ethical conclusions about whistleblowing. Organizational codes of values ("we value integrity") are aspirational; ethical analysis tests whether actual conduct honors them.

Descriptive, normative, and metaethics

Moral philosophy is conventionally divided into three branches, and the distinction is one of the most tested ideas in the course. Descriptive ethics is empirical and value-neutral: it documents what individuals and cultures actually believe and how they behave — the domain of psychology and sociology. Normative ethics is prescriptive: it constructs and defends standards for how people ought to act, yielding the great theories of consequentialism (Mill, 1863), deontology (Kant, 1785), and virtue ethics (Aristotle, trans. 2009). Metaethics investigates the nature and status of moral claims themselves — whether "good" refers to an objective property (moral realism) or merely expresses approval (emotivism). Business ethics is overwhelmingly normative in orientation, drawing on descriptive findings for context and metaethics for foundations. A common student error is to answer a normative question ("should the firm do X?") with a descriptive observation ("competitors all do X") — the is–ought fallacy identified by Hume (1739), which holds that you cannot derive what ought to be purely from what is.

Ethics and the law

Law and ethics overlap but are not coextensive. Law represents a society’s codified minimum; ethics often demands more. The relationship can be mapped onto a 2×2 grid: legal and ethical (routine fair dealing), legal but unethical (exploiting tax loopholes, predatory but lawful lending), illegal but ethically defensible (principled civil disobedience, some forms of whistleblowing that breach confidentiality), and illegal and unethical (fraud, bribery). Carr’s (1968) provocative "business is a game" thesis — that bluffing in business is as acceptable as in poker — represents the view that business operates under its own relaxed morality; most scholars reject this, arguing that businesses depend on the same web of trust as the rest of society (Bowie, 1999).

EthicalUnethical
LegalFair dealingPrice-gouging in a crisis
IllegalJustified whistleblowingFraud, bribery

Why business ethics matters

Beyond moral duty, there is an instrumental case: ethical firms accrue trust, reputation, employee commitment, and reduced legal and regulatory risk (Treviño & Nelson, 2017). Yet relying solely on the business case is itself contested — if ethics is valued only when it pays, it collapses the moment it costs (this tension recurs in the CSR lesson). The deeper justification is that business is a human activity embedded in society and therefore subject to moral evaluation like any other.

Worked example

A consumer-tech firm collects more user data than its app needs, sells it to advertisers, and discloses this in a 40-page terms-of-service document users never read. Descriptively, this is industry norm. Legally, the disclosure may satisfy regulation. Normatively, the practice arguably violates the user’s autonomy and the value of "respecting customers" the firm advertises. The ethical analysis: name the value (respect/transparency), identify the level (organizational policy), reject the descriptive defense ("everyone does it"), and recognize that legal compliance is a floor, not vindication. This is exactly the reasoning sequence the course builds toward.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Confusing legal with ethical — the most frequent managerial error.
  • The is–ought (naturalistic) fallacy — inferring what should be from what is commonly done.
  • Treating ethics as merely subjective opinion — ignoring that normative arguments can be more or less rationally defensible.
  • Conflating values with ethics — having good values does not guarantee ethical reasoning under pressure.

Cross-links

This foundation feeds directly into the normative theories that follow: Consequentialism & Utilitarianism (Lesson 2), Deontology & Rights (Lesson 3), Virtue Ethics & the Ethics of Care (Lesson 4), and Justice & Fairness (Lesson 5). The systemic-level questions raised here resurface in CSR & Stakeholder Theory (Lesson 7) and Governance, Globalization & Contemporary Issues (Lesson 8).

concepts.label: Nature of business ethics: descriptive vs normative
